question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the hiero.Client is constructed once in newClient() and reused across all ticker ticks, so that if the operator key is rotated on-chain, Execute(client) continues to authorize transfers using the stale key without re-validating the operator's current authorization?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a SetMaxTransactionFee() call on NewTransferTransaction() so that Execute(client) authorizes the network to charge an unbounded fee from the operator account, effectively allowing the network (or a rogue node) to drain the operator without explicit owner approval of the fee amount?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user who can modify the container's /tmp filesystem write a crafted /tmp/alive file to keep the liveness probe passing while the pinger is in a compromised state, preventing operators from detecting that unauthorized Execute(client) calls are being made?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that submitWithRetry() logs the full transaction ID (txID=%s) to stdout/stderr, and if logs are shipped to a shared logging system, harvest transaction IDs to correlate with on-chain data and identify the operator account's signing patterns for future unauthorized authorization attempts?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_BASE_BACKOFF to '0s' so that backoff() returns 0 and the retry loop in submitWithRetry() calls Execute(client) with no delay between attempts, authorizing a burst of transfers in rapid succession without any per-transfer owner approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: backoff()] Can an unprivileged external user set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_MAX_RETRIES to a value >= 63 so that the bit-shift expression 1<<(attempt-1) in backoff() overflows a signed integer, producing a negative or zero duration that causes time.NewTimer(sleep) to fire immediately, collapsing the backoff and triggering rapid Execute(client) calls without owner approval for each?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NewTransferTransaction() does not call SetTransactionMemo() or SetTransactionID() with a unique value, making every Execute(client) call produce a transaction that is indistinguishable from previous ones, preventing the operator from auditing which specific transfers they authorized?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a SetNodeAccountIDs() call on NewTransferTransaction() so that Execute(client) selects a consensus node automatically, and if the attacker has poisoned the network map via a rogue mirror node, the transaction is routed to an attacker-controlled node that records the signed authorization?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that hiero.AccountIDFromString(cfg.toAccountID) in submitWithRetry() only validates format (not existence or ownership), allowing the attacker to set toAccountID to a non-existent or attacker-owned account and have Execute(client) authorize a transfer to it without the operator verifying the destination?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the missing check for cfg.amountTinybar > 0 (only != 0 is checked in loadConfig()) to pass a negative amount, causing AddHbarTransfer(client.GetOperatorAccountID(), hiero.HbarFromTinybar(-cfg.amountTinybar)) to add a positive credit to the operator and a debit to toID, authorizing an outgoing transfer from toID without toID's owner signing?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that Execute(client) in submitWithRetry() does not call .Sign() with any additional keys, meaning the transaction is authorized solely by the operator key set in SetOperator(), and if that key is a weak or default key, the attacker can forge authorization without the true account owner's consent?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of a .Freeze() + .Sign() pattern before Execute(client) in submitWithRetry(), which means the transaction body is never independently verified or approved by a separate signer before submission, allowing the operator key alone to authorize transfers that should require additional approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user who can set environment variables supply a HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_KEY that is a valid key for a different account than HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_OPERATOR_ID, causing Execute(client) to submit a transaction with a mismatched key-account pair, and if the network accepts it due to an alias or key rotation, authorize a transfer without the correct owner's approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that submitWithRetry() does not check receipt.Status before returning nil on success, so that a TRANSACTION_EXPIRED or INVALID_SIGNATURE status is misread as success, masking a failed authorization and causing the caller to believe a transfer was approved when it was not?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the hiero.Client object passed to submitWithRetry() is shared across goroutines (ticker ticks) without any mutex, causing concurrent Execute(client) calls to race on the client's internal state and potentially submit duplicate or malformed transactions that are authorized without the operator's per-transaction approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the operator private key (cfg.operatorKey) is stored as a plaintext string in the config struct and passed through multiple function calls, increasing the attack surface for memory-scraping or core-dump attacks that extract the key and use it to authorize transfers without the owner's knowledge?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any on-chain allowance verification before Execute(client) to submit a CryptoTransfer that moves funds the operator was never explicitly approved to move on behalf of another account, violating the approval-before-transfer invariant?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that submitWithRetry() does not call .SetTransactionValidDuration() on NewTransferTransaction(), allowing the SDK to use the maximum valid duration, so that a captured signed transaction bytes can be replayed within that window to authorize additional transfers without the operator's re-approval?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger runs as a long-lived process with a static operator key, so that once the key is compromised (e.g., via log leakage of txID patterns), the attacker can continuously authorize transfers on every ticker tick without any session expiry or re-authentication of the owner?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the missing check for whether toID has a receiverSigRequired flag set to true before calling Execute(client), causing the transaction to be submitted and potentially accepted by the network without the receiver's mandatory signature, bypassing the receiver's authorization requirement?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that AddHbarTransfer() is called twice (once for debit, once for credit) without verifying that the sum of transfers equals zero at the application level, allowing a crafted cfg.amountTinybar value to create an imbalanced transaction that the SDK or network might handle unexpectedly, authorizing an unintended fund movement?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the pinger's operator account (default 0.0.2) is a well-known genesis account, and the default operator key is publicly documented, to call Execute(client) with the default credentials and authorize transfers from that account without any owner approval on a network where the default key has not been rotated?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that submitWithRetry() does not validate that cfg.operatorID corresponds to an account that actually exists on-chain before calling Execute(client), allowing the attacker to set operatorID to a non-existent account and probe network behavior or trigger error paths that leak authorization-related information?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the backoff timer in submitWithRetry() uses time.NewTimer(sleep) inside a select with ctx.Done(), and if the context is cancelled exactly when Execute(client) is about to be called in the next iteration, the transaction may still be submitted before the cancellation is detected, authorizing a transfer the operator intended to cancel?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/transfer.go -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NewTransferTransaction() does not set a SetMaxRetry() or equivalent on the SDK transaction object, allowing the SDK's own internal retry logic to re-submit the signed transaction multiple times to different nodes, each time authorizing the transfer without the operator's per-node approval?
